ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Thai BBQ grilled prawns; closest parent 'shrimp food product' (FOODON_03311737) used as anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW covers shrimps and prawns as a category; no specific code for BBQ-grilled preparation exists in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 227144002 covers prawn as a food substance; no specific code for BBQ grilled preparation exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical restaurant serving of Thai BBQ grilled prawns consists of approximately 4 to 5 medium-large whole prawns, weighing around 150 grams including shell-on presentation.

nutrient_source.title: Shrimp, mixed species, cooked, moist heat (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB 15152)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked shrimp (moist heat, NDB 15152 / FDC ID 175180). Thai BBQ grilled prawns are dry-heat prepared with a light marinade; protein and fat values are comparable to cooked shrimp. Carbohydrate and sodium values are adjusted upward modestly to account for typical Thai BBQ marinade ingredients (fish sauce, oyster sauce, palm sugar) based on standard recipe proportions. Energy is recalculated from adjusted macros using Atwater factors. This is an adapted estimate; exact restaurant values will vary.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai BBQ grilled prawns. Prawns and shrimp are high-protein, very low-carbohydrate foods. The University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care) classify seafood and shellfish as having a negligible glycemic response due to near-zero digestible carbohydrate content. A GI of 5 is assigned as a conservative low estimate consistent with the analogous category of cooked shellfish and lean seafood. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (5 × 5.5 × 1.50) / 100 = 0.41, rounded to 0.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from fish sauce and oyster sauce marinade
hypertension.guidance: A 150 g serving of Thai BBQ grilled prawns contributes approximately 780 mg sodium, representing about 34% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; portion control and requesting reduced-sauce preparation are advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and high protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: Thai BBQ grilled prawns have a negligible glycemic index and a glycemic load of approximately 0.4 per serving, making them a suitable high-protein, low-glycemic option for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, provided the marinade sugar content is kept minimal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Dietary cholesterol and sodium from marinade
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Prawns are naturally high in dietary cholesterol (approximately 152 mg per 100 g) and the BBQ marinade adds significant sodium; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and monitor overall daily cholesterol and sodium intake, while benefiting from the low saturated fat and omega-3 fatty acid content of the prawns.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai BBQ grilled prawns (กุ้งย่างบาร์บีคิว, Goong Yang) are a popular Thai seafood dish consisting of whole medium-to-large prawns marinated in a fragrant blend of garlic, lemongrass, coriander root, fish sauce, oyster sauce, palm sugar, and black pepper, then grilled over charcoal or an open flame until lightly charred and cooked through. The dish is characterised by a smoky, savoury-sweet flavour profile with aromatic herbal notes from the lemongrass and coriander. The texture is firm and juicy with a slightly caramelised exterior from the palm sugar in the marinade. In Thailand, grilled prawns are commonly served as a street food or restaurant main course, accompanied by a spicy seafood dipping sauce (nam jim seafood) made from fresh chillies, garlic, lime juice, fish sauce, and sugar. Nutritionally, Thai BBQ grilled prawns are a high-protein, low-fat, low-carbohydrate food. Per 100 g, they provide approximately 128 kcal, 20 g protein, 2.2 g fat, and 5.5 g carbohydrate, with the carbohydrate largely attributable to the marinade. The sodium content is moderately elevated due to fish sauce and oyster sauce. The dish is rich in selenium, phosphorus, vitamin B12, and cholesterol. The glycemic index is negligible, making it suitable for low-GI dietary patterns. Clinically, the dish is appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low glycemic load, but sodium content warrants attention for those managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. In Singapore, this dish appears on Thai restaurant menus and upscale casual dining venues such as The Upper House at Funan.
common_user_queries: Is grilled prawn good for diabetes?

palm sugar: น้ำตาลปี๊บ

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked shrimp data with marinade adjustments; no exact HPB Singapore or FAO/INFOODS SEA entry for Thai BBQ grilled prawns was found. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database category for cooked shellfish. Confidence is medium rather than low because the base shrimp nutrient profile is well-established and the marinade adjustment methodology is defensible.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy source entry for cooked shrimp. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for cooked shrimp; naturally zero for this food type). Nutrient values reflect an adapted estimate combining USDA SR Legacy cooked shrimp base values with a standard Thai BBQ marinade contribution; actual restaurant values at The Upper House - Funan may differ. Sodium is elevated relative to plain cooked shrimp due to fish sauce and oyster sauce in the marinade. The food_name has been normalised to 'BBQ Grilled Prawns' as the canonical English form; 'Flame-kissed BBQ Prawn' is retained as an alias reflecting the field survey menu name. This dish is classified as Thai cuisine; mandatory Thai-script local name is provided. Chinese names are included as supplementary local names relevant to the Singapore context. Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names are not applicable for this Thai dish and are left null. The GI value of 5 is a conservative analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Thai BBQ grilled prawns was found in the University of Sydney GI Database.
